subject: Your AI Second Brain.
body: The Atlas By GenAI Works By GenAI Works Read this article on LinkedIn to join the conversation Read on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/your-ai-second-brain-genai-works-qlxve?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BB7iNjFs9Q%2ByJom6MU1tB0A%3D%3D&midToken=AQGxgEgizesK0Q&midSig=3g3YM5CUDagYk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-ieyi6f~mqs4wo6r~ge-null-null&eid=ieyi6f-mqs4wo6r-ge Welcome back! This edition moves between useful ideas and uncomfortable questions. We start with something practical: how to build your own second brain using Claude and Obsidian. Then we look at ChatGPTвАЩs biggest health-related upgrade so far, and what it could mean for people using AI to understand medical information. After that, we cover a new NIST finding that shows why AI guardrails may never be fully permanent. Finally, we end with a Google DeepMind system that matched physicians across multiple patient visits, raising a bigger question about how AI could support real healthcare over time. In today's Atlas Newsletter: How to build an AI second brain with Claude and Obsidian. Wire the two together into a knowledge system that compounds instead of resetting every session. Improving your health intelligence in ChatGPT. OpenAI says GPT-5.5 Instant reaches frontier-level health performance, free for everyone, with factuality errors down 71%. Your AI guardrails will be bypassed. A NIST proof extends G√ґdel's theorems to AI safety, and the takeaway reshapes how to secure agents. Towards conversational AI for disease management. DeepMind's AMIE matched primary care physicians over multiple visits, and out-scored them on precision and guideline alignment. PS: Want to collaborate with the biggest GTM engine and distribution moat for AI companies? You can feature your tool with us and reach millions. How to build an AI second brain with Claude and Obsidian that gets smarter every day A new guide making the rounds shows how to turn Claude and Obsidian into a compounding knowledge system, so you stop starting every session from a blank chat window. It builds on Andrej Karpathy's LLM Wiki concept. Obsidian handles local storage and Claude acts as the brain reading and writing to it. How do these pieces fit together? вЖТ The stack: Install Obsidian, a free local text store, and Claude Desktop Pro, the engine that reads and writes your files. вЖТ The bridge: Add the Local REST API plugin inside Obsidian, copy the key, and connect the two through Claude's MCP framework with a single add command (claude mcp add-json obsidian-vault). вЖТ The context: Ask Claude to interview you about your goals, projects and working style, then save its summary to a CLAUDE.md file. That file becomes the memory it loads every time, so it never forgets who you are. вЖТ The structure: Create dedicated project folders such as Inputs, Process, Outputs and Feedback, and open them as isolated vaults. That keeps Claude focused on one job at a time instead of wading through everything at once. вЖТ The automation: Save your repeated routines as markdown skills, then use Claude's schedule tab to trigger daily vault maintenance on its own. Voila! The result is an assistant that organizes your files, summarizes your notes and carries your full context from one day to the next. Each session adds to the graph rather than resetting it. Write CLAUDE in the comments and a complete guide will be sent to you. Could a graph library like Graphify make the connections between your notes even more powerful? Share your thoughts. The money has followed. In 2025 close to one of every two venture dollars went into AI. Khosla Ventures saw it early too, putting $50M into OpenAI in 2019 for a stake now worth billions. In AI, the biggest outcomes have gone to people who got in while the company was still small. GenAI Works is at that stage now. Improving your health intelligence in ChatGPT OpenAI is making its strongest health performance yet available to everyone at no cost. The company says GPT-5.5 Instant is a substantial step forward for health questions, reaching frontier-level performance, and because it ships to all free users, the upgrade reaches the widest possible audience. The scale here is the context. OpenAI reports that more than 230 million people every week bring health and wellness questions to ChatGPT, which makes it one of the most common ways people use the tool at all. The company laid out three ways it evaluated the new model. First, on challenging health benchmarks including HealthBench Professional, GPT-5.5 Instant performs near OpenAI's latest Thinking models and beats GPT-5.3 Instant, released just two months earlier, by a wide margin. OpenAI calls it the largest jump in these models since GPT-5 launched. Second, the company built a tough human baseline. Physicians were asked to write responses to representative health conversations with unlimited time and web access. Independent physicians then graded those answers against five criteria: accuracy, communication clarity, completeness, instruction following and decision helpfulness. According to OpenAI, the new model scored higher than both the physician-written responses and older models across all five. Third, and the result OpenAI highlights most, the company runs privacy-preserving monitors on live ChatGPT traffic, billions of health messages a week, to flag possible factuality errors. Those possible errors dropped by 71 percent over the last two months. OpenAI frames the harder parts of health as more than recall. The model also has to recognize what information is missing, catch red flags, communicate clearly and help someone understand what to do next. To shape that, the company says hundreds of physicians worldwide have reviewed more than 700,000 model responses, defining what a good health answer should do. The stated goal is to make health intelligence abundant, something any patient or clinician can access, trust and use throughout their care. One note for you: these are OpenAI's own evaluations rather than independent studies, and an AI health answer is not a substitute for a clinician. If this touches a personal health concern, it's worth raising with a professional. WDYT? Your AI guardrails will be bypassed NIST just put a mathematical proof behind something security teams have suspected for years: Your AI guardrails will be bypassed. NIST announced a peer-reviewed proof by senior scientist Apostol Vassilev, published in IEEE Security & Privacy under the title "Robust AI Security and Alignment: A Sisyphean Endeavor?" The result is blunt. No finite set of guardrails can be universally robust against adversarial prompts. As NIST's own announcement puts it, "There will always be ways to prompt the AI that can make it disregard these rules." The proof extends G√ґdel's incompleteness theorems to AI safety. G√ґdel showed that any finite, sufficiently expressive logical system contains true statements it cannot prove. Vassilev formalizes a guardrail as a checker function that decides whether a given prompt violates an intended constraint, then proves that for any such finite checker, some constraint will always be enforced incorrectly against some input. Natural language is, in his framing, infinitely ambiguous. The same intent can be expressed in an inexhaustible number of phrasings, encodings and indirect forms. A finite rule set cannot cover all of them. The gap is structural, and no amount of engineering closes it within the same paradigm. The empirical record was already pointing this way. A "refuse-then-comply" fine-tuning attack stripped safety controls from frontier models in controlled testing, succeeding 72% of the time against Claude Haiku and 57% against GPT-4o. Prompt injection has held the top spot on OWASP's LLM Top 10 across both its 2023 and 2025 editions. What the field lacked was a theorem. Now it has one. Here is the part most coverage misses. The answer to a proof that guardrails are incomplete is not better guardrails. NIST's own three-part response model says continuous red-teaming, continuous control updates, and operational resilience for when evasion succeeds. That last element is the uncomfortable one. Evasion will succeed eventually. The math guarantees it. So, the defensible architecture inverts the question. Instead of asking whether the malicious prompt got through, since mathematically it eventually does, you ask: What did the agent do next? Whether that behavior was normal for its role? How fast can you contain it? That is a runtime problem: every agent action observed, behavioral baselines per agent, detection at the action layer rather than the prompt layer, and containment measured in seconds rather than audit cycles. Prevention is now provably incomplete. The behavioral control plane is where AI security actually lives, and NIST just did the math to prove it. Would you know what your AI agent did the moment it happened? Towards Conversational AI for Disease Management A Google DeepMind AI matched primary care physicians at managing

subject: Prime Day is live, shop top deals now, plus the AI agent innovations announced at AWS Summit
body: Amazon News By Amazon By Amazon Read this article on LinkedIn to join the conversation Read on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/prime-day-live-shop-top-deals-now-plus-ai-agent-innovations-announced-5ixoc?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BAxfQp01%2BSvenZPihcW3r5w%3D%3D&midToken=AQGxgEgizesK0Q&midSig=1ikQO-AzrJfck1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-ieyi6f~mqqdlc51~h3-null-null&eid=ieyi6f-mqqdlc51-h3 Want the biggest Amazon stories delivered to your inbox weekly? Subscribe to Amazon News. Best Prime Day 2026 deals: 80+ top picks to shop now Prime Day 2026 kicks off now bringing four days of millions of exclusive deals for Prime members across more than 35 categoriesвАФfrom tech and home to beauty, fashion, and groceries. We've hand-picked over 80 of the best deals from brands like Dyson, Samsung, LEGO, and Le Creuset, and we'll be updating the list throughout the event as new deals drop as often as every five minutes. The global shopping event kicks off on June 23 across 22 countries, and Prime members in Australia, Brazil, India, and Japan can shop Prime Day deals later this summer. Shop top deals now Next: 9 Amazon AI features to help you find the best deals this Prime Day AWS Summit New York 2026: New ways to make AI agents more effective at work AWS announced major AI innovations at its Summit in New York that help customers put agents to work more effectively and create momentum across global organizations. The new AWS Continuum is an AI-native security service that continuously discovers, prioritizes, validates, and remediates security risks. AWS Context provides a comprehensive knowledge graph that gives agents access to the information they need to provide the right answer or take the right next step, faster. AWS also launched additional capabilities across Amazon Quick, Kiro, AWS DevOps Agent, AWS Transform, and Amazon Bedrock AgentCore, including Kiro on mobile for iOS and Amazon Quick autonomous agents, to expand what customers can do with agents. See all the announcements AWS Graviton5 is now generally available, delivering purpose-built performance for the agentic AI era AWS Graviton5, Amazon's most powerful CPU ever, is now available to all customers. The new chip delivers up to 25% better performance than the previous generation, with 192 cores designed specifically for AI applications that take action in real time. Companies like Meta, Uber, and Snowflake are already using Graviton to power their AI workloads. With Graviton5, web applications run 35% faster, machine learning inference is 35% quicker, and databases operate 30% faster than before. The chip is also more energy efficient while delivering better performance, helping businesses meet their sustainability goals without sacrificing speed or capability. See how it works Amazon's AI SVP on why AI's biggest breakthroughs haven't happened yet At VivaTech 2026 in Paris, Amazon SVP Peter DeSantis shared his perspective: AI needs a couple more orders of magnitude of improvement before it becomes truly transformative. Future AI models will need to react as rapidly and dynamically as human interaction, making sense of our movements, stammering, and other subtleties. subject: Claude fixes you wish you knew before.
body: The Atlas By GenAI Works By GenAI Works Read this article on LinkedIn to join the conversation Read on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/stop-wasting-your-claude-limit-genai-works-louye?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BQad1ZyDiRTKTzTB9FPEnMA%3D%3D&midToken=AQGxgEgizesK0Q&midSig=0h75y2iljPeck1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-ieyi6f~mqpdmpow~s2-null-null&eid=ieyi6f-mqpdmpow-s2 Welcome back! Four stories this week, one thread running through them: how we package intelligence and what we ask it to be. We open with a practical guide to squeezing more out of every Claude session, then Google's new open format for feeding agents the context they need, Midjourney's leap from generating images to scanning real bodies, and research that found making models smarter can make them worse at acting human. In today's Atlas Newsletter: 21 ways to avoid hitting your Claude limit: Most people do not need more Claude usage. They need better Claude habits. These 21 fixes show how to save context, choose the right model, batch your work, and know when to start fresh. Google introduced the Open Knowledge Format: Google introduced OKF, a fully open standard for packaging the internal context AI agents need.Instead of relying on an SDK or proprietary account, OKF uses a simple directory of markdown files. Google CloudвАЩs Knowledge Catalog can already ingest it and serve that knowledge to agents. Midjourney is moving into healthcare: Midjourney, best known for AI image generation, unveiled Midjourney Medical and a full-body ultrasonic scanner. The company says the scanner can create MRI-quality 3D body maps in about 60 seconds, without radiation. How to build foundation models for human behavior simulation: A new CMU-led project called OdysSim found something counterintuitive: as models become more capable, they often become worse at imitating real people. Why? PS: Want to collaborate with the biggest GTM engine and distribution moat for AI companies? You can feature your tool with us and reach millions. 21 ways to avoid hitting your Claude limit Most people hit their Claude limit way before they should, then assume they need to pay for more. The truth is messier. The limit isn't the problem. The way you're spending it is. Every re-uploaded PDF, every rambling thread, every "summarize this again" burns context you didn't need to spend. Stack enough of those small habits and you're out by lunch. We pulled together 21 fixes that stretch how far your Claude usage goes, from picking the right model to batching tasks to knowing when to start fresh. Run through them and most people get noticeably more done before they ever see a wall: #1 Convert files before uploading. Don't upload PDFs as screenshots or PPTX if you can avoid it. Copy the text into Google Docs, save it as .md, and upload that instead. #2 Plan in Chat, then execute in Cowork. Don't open Cowork and say "build me a financial model." Start the planning in Chat first, then move to Cowork with "take this file exactly." #3 Make Claude ask you first. Write: "Before executing the task, ask me 10 sequential questions." This saves context from the start. #4 Stop re-reading everything. If Claude makes a mistake, say: "Edit only the third section, don't touch anything else." Add: "no explanation," "output only." #5 Edit your original message. Press Edit on your old messages in Chat instead of sending a new one. Claude replaces the old text rather than stacking extra context. #6 Reuse the same prompt. Keep a prompt library. Change only the variable part. Use the same base prompt for 80% of your work. #7 Batch tasks in one message. Instead of: summarize, extract points, suggest a title (as separate requests), send one: "summarize, extract the points, and suggest a title." #8 Pick the right model. Linguistic check or reformatting? Use Haiku or Sonnet. Read Claude Extended Thinking for heavier work. #9 Keep your files short. Cowork reads the whole folder before each task. Trim files under 2,000 words where possible. Excess words burn tokens. #10 Restart instead of following a broken thread. If Claude is stuck on an error, use: "Restart conversation from here." Want more ways to get more out of Claude every week? Subscribe here. AI companies are going public at record prices and you can get in on this one early Most people only get to buy into AI companies after they go public, once the big money is already in. But everyone knows that investing early gets you higher chances of benefiting more. GenAI Works already earns real revenue and it increased by 2.5x in a year, running go-to-market for AI brands like Nvidia, Oracle, Google and IBM. Our last round raised $520K, and those backers got in first. The growth engine is ToneUp, our enterprise AI platform for brand visibility and campaign performance. It runs on audience and content data from a 14M-strong AI community the rest of the market can't reach, the kind of moat that's hard to copy. This round funds scaling it. Invest from $1,000. Commit by July 1, 2026 and Phase 1 gives you bonus shares: 5% at $1,000, up to 25% at $25,000. After July 1 the same check earns fewer shares, so right now is the best price in the round. Prior investors stack an extra 10%. Invest today In making an investment decision, investors must rely on their own examination of the issuer and the terms of the offering, including the merits and risks involved. Genai Works, Inc. has filed a Form C with the Securities and Exchange Commission in connection with its offering, a copy of which may be obtained here. Google introduced Open Knowledge Format рЯФ• OKF is a new open standard for the context AI agents actually need, and it's fully open. No SDK, no integration, no proprietary account. How does it work? It takes the LLM-wiki pattern Andrej Karpathy, the Director of AI at Tesla, described and turns it into a portable format anyone can produce and anyone can read. Karpathy's observation was simple. LLMs don't get bored, don't forget to update a cross-reference and can touch 15 files in one pass. The bookkeeping that makes humans abandon their wikis is exactly what agents are good at. Here's the problem it solves. The information your models rely on is overwhelmingly internal like Table schemas, what a metric means at your company, the runbook for an incident, the join path between two systems, etc. Today that knowledge is scattered across catalog APIs, random wikis, code comments and the heads of a few senior engineers. So every agent builder solves the same context-assembly problem from scratch. Every catalog vendor rebuilds the same data model. The knowledge stays trapped behind whichever tool created it. You've watched this pattern reappear under a dozen names already such as CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md files. Obsidian vaults wired to coding agents. Repos full of index.md files an agent reads before it does any real work. As a result: same idea, rebuilt every time, and none of them were designed to talk to each other. OKF formalizes that pattern into one portable format. It's a directory of markdown files where each concept is one file and the file path is its identity. вЖТ Just files you can ship as a tarball or host in any git repo. вЖТ Just markdown, readable in any editor and rendered on GitHub. вЖТ Just YAML frontmatter for the queryable fields like type, title and description. Concepts link to each other, so the directory becomes a graph. It asks one thing of every concept: a type field. Everything else is yours. To make it real, the team shipped working implementations at both ends. An enrichment agent that walks a BigQuery dataset, drafts a concept doc for every table and view, then fills each one in with citations, schemas and join paths. And a static HTML visualizer that turns any bundle into an interactive graph in a single self-contained file, with no backend and no data leaving the page. There are three sample bundles you can browse today. Google Cloud's Knowledge Catalog already ingests OKF and serves it to agents. The reason your agents keep relearning the same facts comes down to a missing format. OKF is an attempt to fill that gap. WDYT? Midjourney is moving into healthcare The company behind one of the most popular AI image generators unveiled Midjourney Medical, and its first product is a full-body scanner. The pitch is a machine as powerful as an MRI and as casual as a trip to the spa. How does the Midjourney Scanner work? You step into a shallow pool of golden light and a platform lowers you into the water at about two inches per second. As you descend, you pass through a ring of roughly half a million tiny elements, each the size of a grain of sand and each acting as both a speaker and a microphone. They send ultrasonic waves through your body from every angle and record the ripples back millions of times per second. The waves change shape as they pass through skin, fat, muscle and bone, and reconstructing those changes produces a detailed 3D map of what's inside. No radiation, no magnets, no enclosed tube. Just sound waves and water. The goal is a full scan in about 60 seconds, at close to a hundred times the speed of today's MRI, with the raw reconstruction enhanced by Midjourney's image AI for added detail. The volume of data is the wild part. The company says the array produces terabytes per second. Converted to HD video, you'd watch 500 hours of footage for every one second of scan data. Thousands of computers split the job of turning waves into images. Then there's the delivery. Midjourney plans to house these scanners inside Midjourney Spa locations, complete with hot tubs, saunas and cold plunges, open 24/7. The idea is a place you'd want to go anyway, where the scan is almost a side effect and you walk out with a growing library of data about your own body. The first flagship is set for San F

subject: You never stop cultivating taste
body: The Long & Short of It By Figma By Figma Read this article on LinkedIn to join the conversation Read on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/you-never-stop-cultivating-taste-figma-e3ggc?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BTAQRnXF0SoyCyd6vC%2Fk62g%3D%3D&midToken=AQGxgEgizesK0Q&midSig=3WGSYUQv_4bsk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-ieyi6f~mqmjqjk7~18-null-null&eid=ieyi6f-mqmjqjk7-18 Having the right tools can only take you so far, says Figma Chief Design Officer Loredana Crisan. To become a master of your craft, you need to hone a point of view. No matter your medium, it takes time to master your craft: time to understand how your material behaves, time to learn the mechanics of molding it, and time to pick up the relevant tools. AI doesnвАЩt change this. ThatвАЩs because becoming an expert isnвАЩt just acquiring new skills or techniques; itвАЩs curating a unique way of seeing. This is the most time-intensive part of allвАФin fact, it never ends. My own creative journey began when I studied piano and music composition and became a sound engineer. As a student, I wasnвАЩt just playing notes on a sheet. I was learning to make the phrases my own to reach the person listening, to make them feel something. How could I control rhythm to create moments of suspension or build momentum? How could I use dynamics to shape gradual swells or dramatic peaks? Most importantly, why was I making those choices? Just like playing the piano, design is something you learn through practice. You have to put in the repsвАФby working with mentors, reacting to feedback, and developing a creative intuition that you sustain through consistent care and attention. Taste is a form of care The more we talk about the importance of вАЬtaste,вАЭ the more it starts to lose its meaning. Still, taste is discernible: You know it when you see it. When we recognize something intentional, refined, cultivatedвАФwe say its creator has taste. Taste is built on a unique point of view, and the skill to express it. When you see examples of exceptional tasteвАФsay, Dieter RamsвАЩ work at BraunвАФyou donвАЩt just see the designerвАЩs intent, but his commitment to seeing it through. He didnвАЩt just set out to make a radio; he considered where it would be placed in a room, the objects that would surround it, how it would feel to reach for it. He turned an appliance into an experience. Having taste doesnвАЩt mean everyone will love your work. Taste is particular. Two designers can have completely different sensibilities and both have taste. What they share is the care and intentionality behind every decision. In the world of product design, taste becomes visible in how you navigate trade-offs: form and function, expressivity and legibility, what to add and what to leave out. The details you choose to invest in, the compromises you refuse to makeвАФthatвАЩs where your taste lives. So where does taste come from? It starts with love for your craft. I loved music so much that I spent years developing my own way of hearing. It became something embodied: my fingers recognizing the keys, my ear locking onto the space between notes. In design, that same love shows up when my eyes catch a misalignment, or I respond viscerally to the flow of a page. You first love your craft, then you deepen it, until youвАЩve made something youвАЩre proud of. This doesnвАЩt happen in a vacuum, which is why design critiques and collaborative work are so crucial to making something great. The second ingredient is care for the people youвАЩre reaching. As a sound engineer, I would listen to songs I produced on many different types of speakers before calling a master done. As a designer, I look at different screen sizes, color profiles, and interface languages. Taste means sweating the details most people donвАЩt even see: the transition thatвАЩs a beat too slow, the empty state only a fraction of your users might experience once. When IвАЩm hiring for taste, I look for three things: Discernment: Can they articulate whatвАЩs not working and why? A good answer is nuanced, reflecting a deeper way of seeing than most. Great designers have language for things most people only feel. Empathy: Do they think about the person on the other sideвАФnot just the screen? The tell is when they describe an interface decision that accounted for something you wouldnвАЩt have thought to ask about. Creative energy: Are they always making something? People with taste have a compulsive relationship with their craftвАФside projects, obsessions, things they built because they couldnвАЩt stop thinking about a problem. In the end, taste is care: caring for the user, caring for your craft, and caring about the end result. ItвАЩs a continual process of discerning between what lands and what falls flat, sweating the details, and being relentless about what youвАЩre making and who youвАЩre making it for. AI deepens your creative reserves Some people worry that AI can abstract away the thinking and labor that makes someone truly good at their craft, but that scenario supposes that we accept the first output as final. Doing so would be unacceptable to someone with a strong sense of taste. Sir James Dyson, for example, is famous for having gone through 5,127 prototypes before landing on the iconic design for the Dyson vacuum. The right tools close the gap between what you imagine and what you can make. In the recording studio, an SSL 9000 J console gets you a very different mix than a consumer setup; in design, a powerful design system or a fine-control drawing tool gives you the resolution to render your intent. The vision comes from you. AI can help you explore more broadly, but it canвАЩt stand in for your taste. This is true for every craft or calling. Thinking back to my early days as a pianist, what made the music mine was never playing the correct notes. It was taste. And taste, no matter the medium, is always the same thing: the accumulated weight of intentional choices, repeated over and over again, until what youвАЩve made could only have come from you. subject: How to build an entire business using Claude?
body: The Atlas By GenAI Works By GenAI Works Read this article on LinkedIn to join the conversation Read on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/how-build-entire-business-using-claude-genai-works-kheme?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BcEabhv%2F0Rv2Bp6yYJKl8%2BA%3D%3D&midToken=AQGxgEgizesK0Q&midSig=2FMcHPJspCack1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-ieyi6f~mql67ty3~x9-null-null&eid=ieyi6f-mql67ty3-x9 Welcome back! Ever notice when a technology stops assisting people and starts becoming the system itself? That happened four times this week. PwC rebuilt finance around Claude, Anthropic made AI work instantly shareable, Allbirds reinvented itself as an AI infrastructure company, and a medical AI agent moved from diagnosis to action. In today's Atlas Newsletter: PwC rebuilt finance around Claude: Insurance underwriting dropped from 10 weeks to 10 days after redesigning the process around AI instead of adding AI to an old workflow. Claude Code removes the communication tax: Artifacts turn AI sessions into live, shareable reports so teams spend less time explaining and more time shipping. Allbirds quit shoes for AI infrastructure: A company once worth nearly $4B has reinvented itself as Smartbird, betting its future on GPU infrastructure instead of sneakers. AI is moving from diagnosis to action: A new medical AI agent doesn't just recommend treatments. It orders tests, prescribes medications, schedules procedures and coordinates care. PS: Want to collaborate with the biggest GTM engine and distribution moat for AI companies? You can feature your tool with us and reach millions. PwC built an entire business unit around Claude Pause for a second. PwC didn't only adopt Claude. It built a full AI business unit around it: The Office of the CFO, already live in production. At launch, the CEO of Anthropic Dario Amodei shared the number that matters: insurance underwriting went from 10 weeks to 10 days. Same regulations. Same compliance. Same data. Different systems. That is the lesson. Most companies add AI on top of an old process. PwC rebuilt the process around AI. That is why the result is 10 days and not 10 weeks. The partnership now extends across finance, healthcare and life sciences, using Claude Cowork, Claude Code and the Claude Developer Platform, powered by Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6. вЖТ In finance, that means liquidity forecasting, scenario modeling and capital markets intelligence. вЖТ In healthcare, it means utilization management, care coordination and revenue cycle. вЖТ In life sciences, it means target identification, regulatory submissions and manufacturing quality. PwC is also building industry-specific skills, connectors and plugins on top of Claude, with role-based oversight and human-in-the-loop controls from day one. This is not another AI pilot. It is AI moving into systems of record, where real business decisions happen. The scale is already clear: 30,000 PwC professionals in the U.S. were certified on Claude last month. Hundreds of thousands more are expected globally. And PwC is not alone. Three of the Big Four, PwC, Deloitte and KPMG, have now adopted Claude in production. EY has chosen Microsoft. The most risk-averse firms in professional services have already made their move. How fast can everyone else redesign their systems before the gap becomes visible? Who actually got rich off SpaceX's $1.7 trillion IPO? The investors and engineers who backed it more than two decades ago, when three Falcon 1 rockets had failed in a row and the company was nearly out of money. They own a piece of one of the most valuable companies on the planet today. For all the years SpaceX stayed private, that piece was a club only venture capitalists and insiders could join. The public got its first shot last week. Equity crowdfunding changed that. You can now own a stake in a private AI company before it ever lists. That company is GenAI Works. Real revenue, up 2.5x YoY in 2024-2025. Nvidia, Google, Oracle, IBM and 300-plus partners behind it. Built on the world's largest AI community, 14 million people. GenAI Academy launched and hit 30,000 students in its first month. Our last raise pulled in $520K, and those backers got the first seats. You can take one of the next seats from $1,000. Lock in before July 1 and Phase 1 adds bonus shares, 5% at $1,000 rising to 25% at $25,000, with an extra 10% if you've backed us before. After July 1 that same check buys fewer shares. 12 days left at this price. Reserve your share today In making an investment decision, investors must rely on their own examination of the issuer and the terms of the offering, including the merits and risks involved. Genai Works, Inc. has filed a Form C with the Securities and Exchange Commission in connection with its offering, a copy of which may be obtained here. The engineering team Silicon Valley can't afford to ignore What if your engineering team was open source? AgentField is the open-source AI agent control plane. It orchestrates fleets of agents - including a complete engineering team - across the full code lifecycle. Free open-source nodes you can clone and run today: вЖТ Code Generation ¬Ј Code Review ¬Ј Security Review вЖТ Cloud Security ¬Ј Testing ¬Ј Deployment All running on open models you host. Kimi. MiniMax. GLM. DeepSeek. Or any open weights you bring. Learn how we built the entire multi harness software engineering team вЖТ See it on GitHub How does Claude save teams 10+ hours a week in communication tax? AI isn't the bottleneck anymore. Explaining what the AI did is. You've probably seen it. Claude Code spends an hour debugging, reviewing a pull request or investigating an incident. Then someone asks: вЖТ What did it find? вЖТ What changed? вЖТ What still needs attention? вЖТ Where's the evidence? The work is finished. The explanation is just beginning. Anthropic's new Artifacts feature is designed to remove that step. Instead of leaving the work trapped inside a terminal session, Claude Code can publish it as a live web page. A debugging report. A PR walkthrough. A release checklist. An incident timeline. The page is generated from everything Claude already used: your code, your connectors, your conversation. As Claude keeps working, the page updates automatically at the same link, complete with version history. In Anthropic's own testing, debugging became one of the strongest use cases. An engineer starts an incident investigation before stand-up. Claude analyzes the logs, identifies suspect commits, builds a timeline and publishes a report. By the time the team meets, everyone is looking at the same source of truth instead of waiting for a verbal recap. AI is generating documentation that everyone else can immediately understand. As AI agents become teammates, communication matters as much as execution. Artifacts do more than produce prettier reports. They remove the collaboration tax that shows up after every successful AI session. The takeaway: The next challenge for AI is making their work instantly understandable to everyone else. WDYT? Allbirds quit shoes to become an AI infrastructure company This may surprise you. While everyone is watching the World Cup, Allbirds is becoming an AI infrastructure company. The company now trades as Smartbird. It has sold the Allbirds brand and footwear assets and is now rebuilding itself as an AI infrastructure provider. Its new business: leasing high-performance, low-latency compute to enterprises. The same company that sold wool sneakers last year now wants to compete with companies like CoreWeave and Crusoe. The fall was sharp. After going public in 2021, Allbirds was worth nearly $4 billion. By early 2025, its market value had dropped below $20 million. Then in April, it announced the pivot: Stop selling shoes. Start selling AI infrastructure. Some investors called the move вАЬbizarreвАЭ and вАЬridiculous and concerning.вАЭ Wall Street reacted differently. The stock briefly jumped 800% before giving back much of the gain. On June 17, Smartbird named Nadia Carlsten as president and CEO, replacing Joe Vernachio, who is leaving the company and the board. Carlsten previously ran GPU compute company DCAI, helped launch a sovereign AI supercomputer with NVIDIA and helped build AmazonвАЩs quantum computing service at AWS. She is also joining the board, alongside new chair Lily Yan Hughes. Her view on the rebrand is direct: вАЬIn a few months, people wonвАЩt even remember the shoes.вАЭ She also said she has never worn sneakers: вАЬIвАЩm more of a high heels person myself.вАЭ Her strategy is clear. Smartbird will targeting the mid-market, like: Pharma, Financial services, Countries building sovereign AI, etc. These customers want single-tenant GPU clusters they can own and control. They also want more AI infrastructure without building it all themselves. The market is paying attention. Smartbird doubled its convertible financing facility from $50 million to $100 million. BIRD shares jumped as much as 50% to $5.92, with trading volume above 41 million shares. That is far above normal activity for the small-cap stock. The key point: This is a company that lost most of its value after a nearly $4 billion IPO, then rallied hundreds of percent by pointing itself at AI compute. Now the open question is simple: Is this a real AI infrastructure comeback, or just another AI narrative trade? While the world watches the group stage, one of retailвАЩs strangest pivots is happening off camera. Can AI move from diagnosis to action? For two years, medical AI has been a talker. It reads a chart, answers a question, suggests a differential. Then a human does the actual work: orders the labs, books the surgery, writes the prescription. A new Nature paper closes that gap. subject: NVIDIA and Coherent Help Scale AIвАЩs Optical Backbone in Texas
body: NVIDIA Newsroom By NVIDIA By NVIDIA Read this article on LinkedIn to join the conversation Read on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/nvidia-coherent-help-scale-ais-optical-backbone-texas-nvidia-hhp3c?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3B%2Fu8anXczS8GNIuOLuNh0MQ%3D%3D&midToken=AQGxgEgizesK0Q&midSig=1fDTap-8fL9Yk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-ieyi6f~mql2d1es~xg-null-null&eid=ieyi6f-mql2d1es-xg Follow @NVIDIANewsroom for real-time updates from NVIDIA. Coherent Breaks Ground on Expanded Texas Facility, Scaling AIвАЩs Optical Backbone. Coherent has broken ground on an expanded manufacturing building at its Sherman, Texas, campus, scaling production at what it calls the worldвАЩs first volume production 6-inch indium phosphide fab. The facility supplies key optical components across NVIDIAвАЩs AI stack, including the wafers, lasers and transceivers that help move data between chips, servers and data centers. NVIDIA founder and CEO Jensen Huang joined Coherent CEO Jim Anderson at the ceremony, where the companies highlighted the growing role of optics in modern AI infrastructure. As AI systems expand across racks and data centers, connectivity becomes as critical as compute, with optical networking helping carry data where copper canвАЩt efficiently reach. The expansion is also a milestone for U.S.-based advanced manufacturing. Coherent announced a $50 million CHIPS Act grant tied to the Sherman facility, building on earlier Texas support and NVIDIAвАЩs broader commitment to produce up to $500 billion of AI infrastructure in the U.S. through industry partnerships. Read the full NVIDIA blog to learn how Coherent and NVIDIA are helping build the optical backbone of the AI era. Fastest, Largest, Strongest: NVIDIA Blackwell Sweeps MLPerf Training 6.0. NVIDIA Blackwell led across every category in MLPerf Training 6.0, the latest peer-reviewed industry benchmark for AI training performance. The NVIDIA platform delivered the fastest training times across all seven benchmarks, including new mixture-of-experts workloads reflecting the growing importance of models like DeepSeek-V3 671B and GPT-OSS-20B. The results also showed Blackwell scaling to massive training runs, including 8,192 GPUs using NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 systems on DeepSeek-V3 671B, the largest Blackwell-based MLPerf Training submission to date. NVIDIA also submitted results with GB300 NVL72, which delivered up to 1.6x faster training than GB200 NVL72 at the same scale. For frontier model builders, the results highlight how NVIDIA brings together performance, scale and reliability in a platform designed to help teams train larger models faster, reduce costs and move from training runs to production AI more quickly. Read the full NVIDIA blog for the MLPerf Training 6.0 results and the optimizations behind them. France Advances EuropeвАЩs AI Future With NVIDIA Technologies. A year ago at NVIDIA GTC Paris at VivaTech, France laid out plans to advance local AI вАФ from new AI factories and national compute capacity to open frontier models and industrial platforms. Now, that AI infrastructure is coming online, with AI agents running in production, startups deploying applications, and the French AI ecosystem developing models, datasets and platforms designed around local languages, cultural context and European requirements. The momentum includes MistralвАЩs first deployment of 18,000 NVIDIA GB200 systems, Scaleway offering NVIDIA Blackwell B300-SXM instances, and Bull and Foxconn announcing production of NVIDIA Vera Rubin NVL72 in Europe. NVIDIA technologies are also supporting open model development and production AI use cases across healthcare, telecom, manufacturing, energy and creative industries. Read the full NVIDIA blog to learn how France is turning its AI ambitions into production reality. More News You Missed NVIDIA Blackwell Leads on First Agentic AI Infrastructure Benchmark. HPE AI Factory With NVIDIA Expands for the Era of Agents. How the UK Is Turning Sovereign AI Ambition Into Action With NVIDIA Technologies. At Cannes Lions, NVIDIA Partners Reshape Advertising and Marketing With AI. Hands Free, AIs Forward: NVIDIA XR AI Brings Agents to AR Glasses. For Robotaxis, Safety Must Be Built In, Not Bolted On. In The News Congratulations to Kari Briski, VP of Generative AI Software Products, named one of Fast CompanyвАЩs AI 20 for 2026. Kari helps developers build and run AI agents at scale, advancing the NVIDIA full-stack AI platform that turns massive streams of data into faster, more capable AI systems. NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang joined Sequoia CapitalвАЩs Konstantine Buhler to discuss building the вАЬdynamo of the intelligence age,вАЭ sharing how accelerated computing, AI infrastructure and full-stack innovation are shaping the next era of technology. WhatвАЩs Ahead Registration for #NVIDIAGTC Berlin is now open. October 20вАУ22, join the global AI community for expert-led sessions, hands-on training, and insights on how AI is transforming every industry. Learn more and register now. Stay Connected The innovation doesnвАЩt stop here. subject: You Finished the Course. Now What?
body: W3Schools Newsletter By w3schools.com By w3schools.com Read this article on LinkedIn to join the conversation Read on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/you-finished-course-now-what-w3schools-com-qwvle?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BvzGRO7%2BqRguBYowArMbrLg%3D%3D&midToken=AQGxgEgizesK0Q&midSig=2Q66UGiiQ48sk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-ieyi6f~mqjb32zg~2t-null-null&eid=ieyi6f-mqjb32zg-2t By Thomas Thorsell-Arntsen There is a moment every person learning to code hits. You finish a course, you feel good, and then you open a blank editor to build something on your own. And you freeze. Not because you are stupid. Not because you picked the wrong language. Because nobody told you this was going to happen. Why the course feel easy but the blank editor does not A course removes the hardest parts of building software without telling you it is doing that. You already know what you are building. When you get stuck, the answer is right there. The path is laid out step by step. A blank editor has none of that. You have a vague idea. You do not know every feature. You do not know where to start. And when something breaks, there is no next step to click. That jump is not a small gap. It is a cliff. Devs actually have a name for it: the cliff of confusion. It is the point where the hand-holding stops and you realize you cannot do it on your own yet. Almost everyone who has ever learned to code has stood at that cliff. Most people assume they are the only one. Your brain is working against you (and that is normal) Here is something most people never learn about learning. Research on memory shows that without reinforcement, you forget roughly 50% of new information within 24 hours. Within a week, up to 90% is gone. This is not a personal failing. It is how the human brain works. It was discovered over a century ago by a psychologist named Hermann Ebbinghaus, and it has been confirmed by research ever since. This is why a course feels clear while you are watching it, but a week later the same concepts feel completely unfamiliar. The knowledge never made it into long-term memory because you did not practice it, apply it, or come back to it. Your brain also has a hard limit on how much it can process at once. Working memory can hold roughly four to nine pieces of information at a time. When you sit down to build something from scratch, you are trying to juggle the problem, the syntax, the logic, the structure, and the debugging all at the same time. That is way more than four things. Your brain literally cannot hold it all. This is not a sign that coding is too hard for you. It is a sign that you are trying to do too much at once. The thing nobody tells beginners There is a framework in education called Bloom's Taxonomy. It describes six levels of learning: remember, understand, apply, analyze, evaluate, create. Most courses keep you at the first two levels. You remember syntax. You understand examples. But you never apply, analyze, or create. And then you wonder why you cannot build anything on your own. It is not because you did not study hard enough. It is because watching someone else build something and building something yourself are completely different cognitive tasks. One is recognition. The other is recall. Recognition is easy. Recall is where the real learning happens. Think about it this way. You can watch someone ride a bicycle and understand every part of how it works. But the first time you get on one, you fall. That is not a knowledge gap. That is a practice gap. What actually works The developers who break through this are not the ones who watch more tutorials. They are the ones who start building before they feel ready. Here is what experienced developers consistently say when asked how to escape this cycle: Build something small. Not a full app. A calculator. A to-do list. Something you can finish in an afternoon. The goal is not to build something impressive. The goal is to experience the full process: starting from nothing, getting stuck, figuring it out, finishing. If setting up a local environment feels like its own obstacle, W3Schools Spaces lets you code and host directly in your browser. No installation, no configuration. Just a blank project and you. Copy something that already exists. Pick a website or app you use every day and try to recreate a small piece of it. You are not going to build something better than what they have. That is not the point. The point is that you have a reference you can look at when you get stuck, and you do not have to make design decisions from scratch. Spaces has ready-made templates for portfolios, blogs, and more. Start from a template if the blank page is too much. Modify it. Break it. Fix it. That is building. When you get stuck, step away. This sounds counterintuitive, but your brain actually works on problems in the background. Researchers call it diffuse mode thinking. The mathematician Henri Poincare famously solved a problem he had been stuck on for weeks not while working, but while stepping onto a bus during a trip. The solution appeared with what he described as "perfect certainty." Your brain keeps processing even when you stop trying. Come back to the same concept multiple times. Not cramming it into one long session, but revisiting it over days and weeks. Each time you return to something you partially forgot, the act of retrieving it strengthens the memory. Spaced repetition is one of the most proven techniques in learning science. This week's practice problem If you want to start closing that gap between tutorials and real building, try this week's problem on W3Schools Practice: Capitalize Words. Take a sentence and capitalize the first letter of every word. It is rated Easy and worth 20 XP. You have 3 days left. What makes practice problems useful is not that they are harder than tutorials. It is that they give you a blank editor, a problem description, and nothing else. No step-by-step walkthrough. No answer to peek at. Just you and the problem. That discomfort you feel? That is the learning happening. There are hundreds of problems in each of the 12 languages. Start with this week's easy one. The difficulty does not matter. What matters is that you are writing code from scratch, not following along. Try a practice problem Track your progress If you create a free account, your solutions are saved, your XP adds up, and you can see how far you have come. Sometimes the best motivation is just looking back and seeing that you have solved more problems than you thought. Create your free account Get full access If you are serious about closing the gap, W3Schools Full Access gives you everything in one place. Every course, every exam, every certification we offer. 45+ certifications across web development, data science, and more. Plus every course we add in the future, automatically included. You pay once and it is yours forever. No subscription. No renewal. No hidden costs. Just lifetime access to the full library. Each course is built around doing, not watching. Hands-on exercises, real coding problems, and exams that actually test whether you understand the material. When you pass, you get a verified certificate you can add to your LinkedIn and portfolio. Learn faster. Learn everything. Keep it forever. Get W3Schools Full Access The bottom line: the gap between courses and building is not a talent problem. It is a practice problem. And the only way to close it is to start building before you feel ready. See you in next week's edition! subject: What Are the Most Common AI Implementation Challenges in Healthcare?
body: The Technology Partner Brief By InApp By InApp Read this article on LinkedIn to join the conversation Read on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/what-most-common-ai-implementation-challenges-healthcare-inapp-ob27c?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3B5SEGkfmfT1OAktB3lAYSrg%3D%3D&midToken=AQGxgEgizesK0Q&midSig=0ydo-p7tCR8ck1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-ieyi6f~mqj8d6ax~k-null-null&eid=ieyi6f-mqj8d6ax-k There is a lot of talk about intelligent systems in healthcare, but the reality is that adoption is difficult. Technical limitations, workforce challenges, and evolving regulations often prevent promising initiatives from moving beyond pilot programs. Here are the most common challenges healthcare organizations face when implementing AI. Technical and Data Challenges Healthcare data is often less reliable than organizations admit. About 80% of healthcare data is unstructured, which makes it difficult for AI algorithms to use. Technical Barriers AI integration in healthcare faces several technical challenges: Re-identification risks: Even when names and Social Security numbers are removed, advanced algorithms can still use information like ZIP codes, birth dates, and medical histories to identify patients. This risk makes privacy protections weaker and can cause compliance and ethical problems. Fragmented systems: Healthcare organizations often work in digital silos. Each department, facility, and vendor uses its own system, and these systems do not communicate with one another. This keeps patient data separated, which makes it hard to build AI models that need complete, unified datasets. Legacy infrastructure: Many healthcare organizations still rely on technology platforms that are decades old. These systems were not built for modern data exchange or to work with new AI tools. Replacing them costs too much, but keeping them makes it nearly impossible to adopt AI in a meaningful way. Model limitations: Algorithms trained on incomplete or biased data often miss important details. Critical injuries, rare conditions, and unusual cases can be missed since the algorithms lacked training on a diverse range of data. What appears comprehensive in theory can prove unreliable in practice. Human and Organizational Barriers Clinicians who use AI often face social resistance. A Johns Hopkins study found that doctors who used AI for decisions were seen as less skilled by their peers, who doubted their abilities. This stigma sticks around even when AI is just used to double-check decisions. The Training Vacuum Gaps in education make it hard to bring AI into healthcare at all levels. Twenty-seven studies found that workforce training is the main barrier, and most medical programs do not teach AI basics. Schools are not set up to add AI education, often because of limited time and not enough teaching expertise. Without special skills, professionals have trouble judging AIвАЩs performance, accuracy, and fit for clinical use. Regulatory and Ethical Challenges Regulatory systems are struggling to keep up with how quickly AI is growing. The FDA has approved almost 1,000 AI-enabled medical devices, but the old system was not designed for flexible algorithms that keep learning and changing. Each update can require a new review, which slows down developers and delays patient care. The Bias Crisis Algorithmic bias creates serious risks when using AI in healthcare. Studies show that 70% of image-based diagnostic AI systems relied on data from only three states, leaving thirty-four states out entirely. One widely used cardiovascular risk algorithm underestimated heart attack risk in women because it did not consider gender-specific symptoms, which led to delayed treatment and preventable deaths. Out of ten FDA-approved breast imaging products, only one reported the racial makeup of its validation data. Transparency Gaps Black-box models make it hard for clinicians and patients to understand how decisions are made. Healthcare providers cannot explain how AI reaches its conclusions, which goes against informed consent. When AI makes mistakes that harm patients, it is unclear who is responsible. Doctors are legally responsible for AI recommendations they may not fully understand, while developers keep their methods secret. Conclusion AI in healthcare faces three main challenges: fragmented data and security risks, resistance from staff due to fear and lack of training, and regulations that have trouble addressing bias and accountability. Organizations that recognize these issues can create better strategies for using AI. Success depends on clear governance, involving clinicians from the start, and building strong data systems. Leaders who tackle these problems head-on will move beyond pilot projects and deliver real AI solutions to patients. subject: This may surprise you: SpaceX is buying Cursor for $60 billion
body: The Atlas By GenAI Works By GenAI Works Read this article on LinkedIn to join the conversation Read on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/may-surprise-you-spacex-buying-cursor-60-billion-genai-works-spuue?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3Bnk%2FCBNavS86UI4aBzCMKPw%3D%3D&midToken=AQGxgEgizesK0Q&midSig=23wkOHKC9b7sk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-ieyi6f~mqicufb6~yx-null-null&eid=ieyi6f-mqicufb6-yx Welcome back! Pause for a second. SpaceX's reported $60 billion Cursor deal shows that owning the developer workspace may now matter more than building another model. A new CVPR paper shows that better image segmentation was already hidden inside frozen vision models. The trick was using the features differently. Databricks launched Omnigent to help different AI agents work together instead of running separately. And Microsoft made Copilot Cowork generally available with usage-based pricing, turning agent work into something companies can track, consume, and meter like cloud computing. In today's Atlas Newsletter: Why SpaceX paid $60 billion for Cursor: the AI war stopped being about the biggest model and became a land grab for where work happens. Segmentation with zero training and one example: a CVPR paper pulls a hidden bias out of frozen features and the accuracy was waiting there all along. Your agents do not talk to each other: Databricks open-sourced Omnigent, a meta-harness that sits above Claude Code, Codex and Pi to compose, control and share them. Microsoft just made agent work a metered utility: Copilot Cowork hit general availability with usage-based pricing, Microsoft's first major pricing shift in nearly two decades. PS: Want to collaborate with the biggest GTM engine and distribution moat for AI companies? You can feature your tool with us and reach millions. Why did SpaceX buy Cursor for $60 billion? Ever notice this pattern? The biggest AI moves lately are not about models. They are about real estate. SpaceX just agreed to buy Anysphere, the company behind Cursor, in an all-stock deal worth about $60 billion. The merger was signed June 16, days after SpaceX's blockbuster IPO. A rocket and satellite company buying a coding assistant sounds strange until you see what they actually bought. They did not buy a model. xAI already has Grok. They bought the place developers sit all day. Cursor's revenue tells the story. It hit a $4 billion annualized run rate, more than double where it sat in February. That growth is not coming from autocomplete. It is coming from owning the surface where engineers decide what to build, what to refactor, what to test and what to ship. Here is the pattern once you start looking for it. Anthropic, OpenAI and Google have spent two years fighting over the same layer. Claude Code, Codex and Cursor keep rewriting how software gets shipped. The model is becoming a commodity. The interface to real work is becoming the prize. SpaceX had a choice spelled out in the original option: pay roughly $10 billion to keep collaborating, or buy the whole thing for $60 billion. They bought the whole thing. When a company pays a 6x premium to own the interface instead of renting it, that is the pattern showing its hand. Own the model and you own a capability. Own the place where work happens and you own the work. Software development is just the first battlefield. The same pattern is coming for design, legal, finance and support. Watch where the surfaces get bought next. The next AI war is a land grab for where work happens. The biggest model is no longer the thing worth owning. By Sunday night, you'll have Claude running your desktop. Anthropic ships new Claude capabilities almost every week. Skills, Connectors, Cowork, vibe coding. Most professionals are still using Claude for one-off chats, missing the modes that actually change how work gets done. Outskill's 2-Day Claude AI Mastery Workshop is a live, end-to-end walkthrough taught by mentors who condensed 800+ hours of research and real-world testing into one weekend. You'll walk away knowing: How to use Claude's three modes: Chat, Cowork, and Code How to set up Skills and Connectors that automate your Notion, files, and desktop How to vibe code working apps and dashboards alongside 10+ tools that pair with Claude Saturday and Sunday, 10 AM to 7 PM EST. Free for the next 48 hours. Save your free spot Learn AI from the people who use it every day AI is rewriting how work gets done, and the gap between people who keep up and people who fall behind is widening fast. GenAI Academy is how you stay on the right side of it. Every course is built and curated by working AI experts, not theorists, so you walk away with moves you can use the same afternoon. Here is what to start with. Claude Starter Course (free) The fastest way from "I opened Claude once" to using it well every day. Set up, prompt, and get real output in an hour. Claude Practitioner's Guide to AI Agents (free) Go past chat. Build agents that take a task and carry it through, with the patterns practitioners actually rely on. How to Stop Hitting Your Claude Limit (free) The limit usually hits mid-task on something small. This course shows you why, then hands you 24 fixes across Claude.ai, Cowork and Claude Code so it stops happening. Mastering Claude Code (early bird offer) The deep track. Advanced prompting, real projects, and the habits that separate casual users from power users. Locked-in pricing for early sign-ups. Claude Excel for Finance (early bird) Turn Claude into a finance analyst that lives in your spreadsheets. Modeling, cleanup and analysis at a pace manual work cannot match. Early-bird pricing while it lasts. Three free courses to start. Two early-bird tracks when you want to go further. Pick one and put it to work this week. Image segmentation work with zero training and a single example This may surprise you. Researchers made image segmentation work with zero training and a single example. Segmenting a new object usually means labeled masks or a fine-tuned model. Both fall apart the moment you switch domains. A new CVPR paper says one example is enough. INSID3 segments anything from a single annotated image. Zero training. Everything runs on frozen DINOv3 features. No decoder. No mask supervision. No SAM. The clever part is what the team found hiding in those features. The patches carried a positional bias. They were matching by location instead of meaning. Two patches looked similar to the model because they sat in the same spot, not because they showed the same thing. The fix is three steps: Feed pure noise through the encoder Pull out the bias direction with SVD Subtract it from the features Clean features then match the example to the target, and clustering builds the final mask from the coherent regions. The payoff: accuracy jumps 7.5% mIoU over the previous best, with 3x fewer parameters, running faster. The same approach handles medical, underwater and aerial images without retraining. Here is the part worth holding onto. The capability was already sitting inside a frozen model. Nobody trained anything new. They found a bias, removed it, and the performance was there waiting. The win came from using the model better, not building a bigger one. That lesson travels well past segmentation. Most people leaving capability on the table are not running a weaker model, they are running one carrying baggage that gets in its own way. What else is hiding inside self-supervised features? Nobody mentions this part of the agent boom: the agents do not work together Omnigent is the open source meta-harness that combines, controls and shares your agents. It lets you build multi-agent coding and custom agents, sitting above Claude Code, Codex, Pi and agent SDKs so you can compose them. It also adds live collaboration and rich control policies. Omnigent came out of what Databricks and Neon saw in practice. Engineers were combining multiple agents into loops, and that was hard to do above the harness layer. The fix was a uniform API above any harness that makes richer features possible on top. For composition, you create multi-agent teams across different harnesses, or swap harness and model mid-session and mid-loop. For collaboration, Omnigent puts a common UI above any agent, with native app, web and mobile, plus live collaboration with teammates. No copy-pasting text and screenshots between agents, Slack and Docs. For security and cost control, Omnigent adds a contextual policy model that tracks session state. It can ask for permission to git push after an agent downloads an untrusted npm package, or pause an agent after every $100 of spend. A strong OS sandbox from the security team backs it. A lot of work is about to move past the individual agent and up to a meta-harness layer like this as agents get more widely adopted. That is why Databricks is open sourcing this foundation, built from real coding and custom agent needs at Databricks and Neon. The project now handles full coding and product management with a single interface to every agent. It is open source under Apache 2.0. Try it and join the community to build it with others. Pause for a second. Microsoft turned agents into a metered utility Copilot Cowork went generally available on June 16, and the pricing change underneath it is the part worth reading twice. It is Microsoft's first major pricing model shift in nearly two decades. After a three-month Frontier preview, more than half of the Fortune 500 are already using Copilot Cowork, along with Accenture, Avanade, Advance Local, Capital Group, Koch, LTM, Ooredoo Qatar and Zurich Insurance. What it does: you define a complex, long-running, multi-tool task and Cowork runs it end to end, returning a finished result instead of a draft. Microsoft points to real examples. subject: Why Unified Platforms Have Become Essential in Manufacturing
body: The Technology Partner Brief By InApp By InApp Read this article on LinkedIn to join the conversation Read on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/why-unified-platforms-have-become-essential-manufacturing-inapp-0o5pc?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3Bvo0kLL%2FZRxe48SGbIEogrQ%3D%3D&midToken=AQGxgEgizesK0Q&midSig=0zWR1M8pDW4sk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-ieyi6f~mqfho42u~ln-null-null&eid=ieyi6f-mqfho42u-ln Imagine your production line suddenly stops, but your maintenance team canвАЩt get real-time equipment data because itвАЩs stored in separate systems. At the same time, quality control finds a defect pattern, but thereвАЩs no quick way to notify production planning. Sound familiar? Issues like these cost the industry billions of dollars every year and create hidden obstacles that prevent manufacturers from reaching their full potential. The Reality of Disconnected Manufacturing One of the biggest challenges for manufacturers today is the ongoing gap between Information Technology and Operational Technology (IT-OT) systems. This split, often called a вАЬcompetitive gapвАЭ by industry experts, slows down efficiency, quality, and innovation. When production data is separated from business systems, manufacturers lose visibility into their operations. When systems are disconnected, teams end up working in silos. Production teams donвАЩt have real-time access to inventory, quality assurance results donвАЩt reach the production floor immediately, and planning teams often find out about supply chain issues too late. This lack of integration leads to extra manual work, slower responses, and missed opportunities for improvement, impacting growth and profits. Want to learn how top manufacturers are tackling these problems? Sign up for our upcoming webinar, вАЬContinuous Modernization in Action: Building a Unified Platform with Site Security.вАЭ Moving Toward Connected Systems In Manufacturing The answer isnвАЩt just adding more technology. To improve efficiency, manufacturers need to fix disconnected workflows by using integrated systems that support connected operations. When everyone works in a shared data environment, information moves automatically without manual transfers or checks. This automation speeds up decisions, reduces paperwork, and improves production accuracy. Manufacturers can spot issues early, optimize resources in real time, and use data to improve quality and output. The real question isnвАЩt if you should modernize your systems, but how soon you can set up unified platforms to close the gap between IT and operations and stay ahead in a fast-changing market. Ready to improve visibility across your manufacturing ecosystem? Join our webinar to see how a unified platform strategy can streamline operations, accelerate decision-making, and drive continuous modernization.
