subject: 4 techniques to optimize your AI work. Stop blaming the model.
body: The Atlas By GenAI Works By GenAI Works Read this article on LinkedIn to join the conversation Read on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/4-techniques-optimize-your-ai-work-stop-blaming-model-genai-works-xpyxe?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BVfvaiZDZQOSVDHH5YTiMNw%3D%3D&midToken=AQGxgEgizesK0Q&midSig=0USYL5-HrB4sk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-ieyi6f~mqfgmdvr~rn-null-null&eid=ieyi6f-mqfgmdvr-rn Welcome back! This edition has a theme running through it: the next leap in AI is coming from the layers around the model, not just the model itself. Better backends, better documents, better hardware use and better loops. We have a developer who cut token usage 2.5x without changing the model, four companies betting the PDF is finished for AI, Google making local AI use your GPU properly and the engineering skill that turns one person into a team. In today's Atlas Newsletter: The hidden token tax: why coding agents burn cash reading backends built for human dashboards, and the open-source fix that cut one bill in half. The end of PDF for AI: IBM, NVIDIA, Red Hat and ABBYY launch DocLang, an AI-native document standard built for machines. DiffusionGemma's real trick: how Google shifted local inference from memory-bound to compute-bound for up to 4x faster generation. Loop engineering: the skill that removes you as the bottleneck and moves you up the stack. PS: Want to collaborate with the biggest GTM engine and distribution moat for AI companies? You can feature your tool with us and reach millions. Why Anthropic coding agents burn tokens on backends built for humans A developer testing Claude Fable 5 on a full-stack RAG app cut token usage by 2.5x by changing one thing: The backend the agent had to understand. The first build used Firebase. It cost 5.5M tokens, required 7 manual interventions and came to $8.94. The rebuild came in at 2.3M tokens, required zero interventions and cost $4.17. Same final product. Very different path to get there. The reason is simple: most backend tools were designed for human eyes rather than for agents. Before writing code, an AI agent needs to understand the system: permissions, storage buckets, auth providers, edge functions, deployment rules and runtime state. A human can click through a dashboard to read all of that. An agent has to pull the same information through API calls. Firebase made that expensive. No single call returned the full picture, so the agent kept querying. Each query returned too much information, much of it irrelevant. Some important state, such as active auth providers, was not queryable at all and had to be supplied manually. Errors made the problem worse. A generic "permission denied" message gave the agent too little context, so it guessed, rewrote code, retried and burned more tokens. The rebuild used InsForge, an open-source self-hostable backend layer designed to expose backend state in a way agents can use. One CLI call, around 500 tokens, gave the agent the full backend topology: auth, database, storage, edge functions, model gateway, micro VMs and deployment. Four narrowly scoped skills activated only when relevant, keeping context small. Every CLI operation returned structured JSON with meaningful exit codes, so retries did not turn into guesswork. The takeaway is clear: The token bill is not always a model problem. Often it traces back to tooling. If your infrastructure is designed for human eyes, agents will waste tokens trying to understand it. Structure the information layer for the agent, and the waste starts to disappear. You can read your competitorвАЩs ChatGPT ad copy right now. ChatGPT has ads now, and the early movers are already in. This month alone, over 1,000 brands are running active campaigns across retail, SaaS, travel, and financial services. Most marketers donвАЩt know itвАЩs happening yet. Trendos Ad Radar shows you exactly whatвАЩs running. See whoвАЩs advertising, read their exact ad copy, and track where theyвАЩre sending traffic. Impression share, messaging, and final URLs are all mapped and searchable. Free today. See whoвАЩs advertising in ChatGPT Anyone can rent a frontier model now. The hard part is reaching the people who buy, and knowing what converts before you spend. GenAI Works has run go-to-market for AI brands including Nvidia, Oracle, Google and IBM, growing annual revenue 2.4x in a year. We're building that capability into ToneUp, an enterprise platform that puts brand voice, content, visuals and performance analytics in one place. It trains on live campaign data from one of the world's largest AI communities, 14 million strong, plus the Academy and our hackathons. Data the rest of the market can't reach. The round is open, and it funds ToneUp's scaling. Invest $1,000 or more by July 1, 2026 for 5% bonus shares, up to 25% at the $25,000 tier. Every tier drops after that date. Explore further In making an investment decision, investors must rely on their own examination of the issuer and the terms of the offering, including the merits and risks involved. Genai Works, Inc. has filed a Form C with the Securities and Exchange Commission in connection with its offering, a copy of which may be obtained here. The document format that survived 30 years didn't survive AI Everyone thinks better AI needs bigger models. IBM and NVIDIA just made a different bet. Maybe the problem was your documents all along. For 30 years, PDF has been the default format for sharing information. This week, IBM, NVIDIA, Red Hat, and ABBYY launched DocLang, an AI-native document standard, effectively arguing that PDF was built for humans, not machines. Here's the hidden tax every AI system pays today: вЖТ Parsers guess at reading order. вЖТ Tables collapse into flat text. вЖТ Figures disappear during ingestion. вЖТ Metadata gets lost. The result? Your model is not reading the document you think it is. DocLang fixes the format instead of the model. Every element carries structure, meaning, location, and reading order in a way AI can understand natively. Tables use OTSL, requiring just 5 structural tokens where HTML needs 28. That's the uncomfortable part. The AI industry spent years building larger models to compensate for documents that were never designed for machines. The next breakthrough may come from giving the AI models better documents. Understanding this shift is becoming a real AI skill. WDYT? How Google made a local LLM compute-bound instead of memory-bound GoogleвАЩs DiffusionGemma guide is out, and the real story is not only speed. It is local AI efficiency. Most LLMs generate one token at a time. On a local GPU, that means the model keeps pulling weights from memory while the GPUвАЩs compute power sits underused. DiffusionGemma changes the pattern. Instead of writing left to right, it starts with a 256-token canvas and refines the whole block in parallel. That gives the GPU more work at once and shifts the bottleneck from memory bandwidth to compute. The result: up to 4x faster generation, with Google reporting 700+ tokens per second on an RTX 5090 and 1000+ on a single H100. The model also has one important advantage over standard autoregressive models: it can revise itself. If a token looks wrong during generation, the system can re-noise and replace it before the final answer is locked. For longer outputs, DiffusionGemma generates in blocks, combining parallel speed inside each block with sequential stability across the full response. The bigger message: The next local AI race may be about models designed to use your hardware properly. DiffusionGemma is still experimental and trades some quality for speed, but it points to a clear direction: faster, cheaper AI that runs closer to the user. Share your toughts. The skill that just made one engineer worth eight Karpathy said something many teams will regret ignoring: "Remove yourself as the bottleneck. Maximize your leverage. Put in very few tokens, and a huge amount of stuff happens on your behalf." That is exactly what loop engineering is for. In a normal AI coding session, the human does two things: Decides what the agent should run next Checks the output before the next step Both steps are manual. Both limit how far the agent can go alone. Loop engineering moves those steps into the system. A schedule decides what to run. A maker agent produces the work. A separate checker agent reviews it. A state file on disk tracks what is done and what is still open. The loop continues until the task is complete, the iteration limit is reached, or the budget runs out. But good loops need guardrails. First, the maker should not grade its own work. A model often justifies what it already produced. A separate checker is needed to find what failed and send that feedback back into the loop. Second, stop conditions must be defined before the loop starts. Without limits, a loop can burn tokens quickly, especially when subagents and long runs are involved. Third, the state should live on disk, not only in context. A simple markdown file or knowledge graph lets the loop resume later without losing track. Fourth, the safer loops are the boring ones. Version checks, missing tests, stale docs, lint issues and repeated validation tasks are easier to verify than judgment-heavy work. The failure modes matter too. A loop can say "done" when nothing meaningful was verified. Green tests only prove the code passed the tests, not that the team understands what shipped. A checker can also keep one run honest while the surrounding harness slowly breaks as models, prompts and tools change. That means loop engineering keeps the human in the picture and moves them higher up the stack. The job becomes designing the loop, setting the limits, reading the traces and knowing when to step back in.
